coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots derived from cellobiose for temperature and phosalone detection
coredata.dc:creator: Liao, Xiufen
Chen, Congjin
Wang, Peiwen
Zhou, Ruxia
Zhao, Xilian
Fan, Hui
Huang, Zuqiang
coredata.dc:description: Cellobiose, the hydrolysis product of cellulose and the dimer of glucose, was employed to synthesize carbon dots (CDs) via a simple hydrothermal method. The inducing of nitrogen can act as a “molecular glue”, more and more carbon clusters were “glued” together, which would push the hydrothermal reaction in the positive direction. Besides, the linear response of the fluorescence of N-CDs to temperature ensured its potential application in temperature sensing. In the temperature range of 10∼80 °C, the fluorescence of as-prepared N-CDs was reversible and recoverable. Moreover, the dual fluorescence centers and dual-absorption bands of the as-prepared N-CDs permitted the novel dual-mode ratiometric assays for phosalone detection. The linear range and limit of detection (LOD) for dual fluorescence center-based ratiometric assay were 0.12∼5.45 μg/mL and 42.90 ng/mL, respectively. While that for the dual absorption-based ratiometric assay were 0.02∼1.40 μg/mL, and 6.67 ng/mL, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Cellobiose
Temperature sensing
Phosalone

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ratiometric detection of phosalone via the dual fluorescence centers of N-CDs
Ratiometric detection of phosalone via the dual absorption bands of N-CDs
Possible formation mechanism of CDs and N-CDs
Comparison of the CDs derived from cellulose and cellobiose

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Absorption and the corresponding fluorescence spectra of CDs at the hydrothermal temperatures ranging from 140 °C to 210 °C (A). Effect of the amounts of cellobiose (0.01 g ∼0.20 g) to the absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of CDs (B). Curves illustrate the absorbance and fluorescence intensity of CDs at the hydrothermal time ranging from 3 h∼12 h (C). Absorption and fluorescence spectra of CDs before and after N-doping (D). Schematic illustration of the proposed mechanism for the formation of HMF, sp2 carbon cluster, and CDs (E). All fluorescence measurements of Figure. 1 (A), (B), and (C) were carried out with slit width of Ex=5 nm, Em=10 nm; and the fluorescence of Figure. 1 (D) was measured with the slit width of Ex=5 nm, Em=5 nm.

TEM image of N-CDs (Inset: distribution curve and HRTEM image) (A); XRD pattern of N-CDs (B); FT-IR curve of N-CDs (C); absorption spectrum (a), excitation spectrum (b), and emission spectra (c: λEx = 230 nm; d: λEx = 320 nm) of N-CDs, inset of figure 2D was the images of N-CDs under daylight and 365 nm UV light, respectively (D).

Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs with the excitation wavelength of 230 nm (A) and 320 nm (B) at different temperatures; Fi/F10 ratio at 420 nm with the excitation wavelength of 230 nm (black) and 320 nm (red) is linear calibration plotted versus temperature ranging from 10∼80 °C (C); The reversible temperature-dependence of the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs between 10 and 80 °C (D).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs excited by 230 nm in the presence of 0.00∼5.45 μg/mL of phosalone. (B) Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs excited by 320 nm in the presence of 0.00∼5.45 μg/mL of phosalone. (C) Absorption spectrum of N-CDs and phosalone. (D) The absorption spectra of N-CDs at various concentrations of phosalone.

(A) The titration curve of the fluorescence ratio of I320/I230 versus phosalone concentrations of 0.00, 0.12, 0.27, 0.38, 0.74, 1.38, 1.94, 2.86, 3.75, 4.44, and 5.45 μg/mL. (B) Plotted the relationship between absorbance ratio of A270/A207 and the phosalone concentrations of 0.00, 0.004, 0.01, 0.02, 0.03, 0.04, 0.10, 0.20, 0.30, 0.40, 0.60, 0.80, 1.00, 1.20, 1.40, 1.60, 1.80 μg/mL, and the linear relationship of A270/A207 absorption ratios versus phosalone concentrations from 0.02 μg/mL to 1.40 μg/mL. Inset: Enlarge the relationship of A270/A207 versus the concentration of phosalone in the range of 0.00 to 0.20 μg/mL.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots derived from cellobiose for temperature and phosalone detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aSchool of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Petrochemical Resource Processing and Process Intensification Technology, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Petrochemical Resource Processing and Process Intensification Technology
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bGuangxi Key Laboratory of Petrochemical Resource Processing and Process Intensification Technology, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cellobiose, the hydrolysis product of cellulose and the dimer of glucose, was employed to synthesize carbon dots (CDs) via a simple hydrothermal method. The inducing of nitrogen can act as a “molecular glue”, more and more carbon clusters were “glued” together, which would push the hydrothermal reaction in the positive direction. Besides, the linear response of the fluorescence of N-CDs to temperature ensured its potential application in temperature sensing. In the temperature range of 10∼80 °C, the fluorescence of as-prepared N-CDs was reversible and recoverable. Moreover, the dual fluorescence centers and dual-absorption bands of the as-prepared N-CDs permitted the novel dual-mode ratiometric assays for phosalone detection. The linear range and limit of detection (LOD) for dual fluorescence center-based ratiometric assay were 0.12∼5.45 μg/mL and 42.90 ng/mL, respectively. While that for the dual absorption-based ratiometric assay were 0.02∼1.40 μg/mL, and 6.67 ng/mL, respectively.

In this paper, cellobiose was employed to synthesize carbon dots via a facile hydrothermal method. The formation mechanism of CDs was discussed in detail.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), the youngest member of the carbon nano material's family, is the zero-dimensional nanomaterial with sizes lower than 10 nm [1,2]. As the rising star of nanomaterials, the synthesis and application of CDs have become one of the research hotpots in terms of its controllable and unique physical, chemical, and derivative properties. At present, CDs have been widely applied in the fields of fluorescence/electrochemical sensor [3,4], bio-imaging [5,6], light-emitting diodes [6], drug delivery [7], anti-counterfeiting [8], photo/electrons catalysis [9,10], light active sensitizer [11], and so on.

Cellulose is the richest biomass polymer in the world and is regarded as the most promising and inexhaustible energy and chemical feedstock to realize the sustainable development of human society in the future [12, 13]. CDs derived from cellulose have been proven to be feasible [14]. Due to its polydisperse linear glucose polymer chains and the formation of hydrogen-bonded supramolecular structure [15], CDs derived from cellulose often require rigorous conditions, such as high energy, long reaction time, high reaction temperature, and strong acid. Cellobiose is the hydrolysis product of cellulose. Compared with cellulose, cellobiose has a simpler structure, which makes it an ideal carbon source for the synthesis of CDs. Therefore, converting cellulose to cellobiose can synthesize CDs under relatively facial conditions and obtain a higher yield, which might be a feasible strategy for CDs synthesis from cellulose.

The raw CDs often suffer from the drawback of low fluorescence efficiency, which may limit their application. Silva Souza et al [16] have obtained CDs from cellulose through sulfuric acid carbonization and nitric acid oxidation steps, while the obtained CDs only presented a low quantum yield (QY) of 1.2 %. Ng et al [17] synthesized green and blue fluorescence CDs from cellulose with the low quantum yield of 6.4% and 4.0%, respectively. In Liu's study [18], they prepared CDs from “cellulosic” biomass via the microwave-assisted hydrothermal (MHTC) method. Although MHTC method can improve the conversion yield of raw materials to CDs and shorten the reaction time, it still suffers from a relatively low QY of 6.20 %. Doping CDs with heteroatoms is the main strategy to effectively modulate their bandgap and electronic density, enhancing chemical activity, and fluorescence of CDs for practical applications [19–21]. However, the formation of CDs and the mechanism of the fluorescence efficiency enhanced by heteroatoms are still keep in debate.

This paper aims to fabricate CDs from cellobiose, discussed its formation, fluorescence enhance mechanism, and explored its potential application. The QY of the as-prepared CDs was effectively enhanced by N-doping (N-CDs). In order to find out the formation and the fluorescence enhancement mechanism, the UV-Vis spectra and fluorescence emission spectra of the as-prepared CDs were investigated. Based on the aggregation-induced emission (AIE) theory [22], the concept of “molecular glue” was introduced to explain the formation mechanism and the fluorescence enhancement mechanism of CDs and N-CDs. Besides, as the temperature raised from 10 to 80 °C, the fluorescence of the N-CDs was quenched gradually. The fluorescence quenching intensity was in a linear relationship with temperature, which indicated its potential application in temperature sensing. Furthermore, the presence of phosalone induced different responses of the dual fluorescence centers and the dual absorption peaks. Based on this, the novel dual- mode ratiometric assays for phosalone detection were constructed. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first time to propose dual fluorescence center-based and dual absorption-based ratiometric assays for phosalone sensing.

Cellobiose (biological reagent, 98%) was obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. NH4HCO3 was obtained from Damao Chemical Reagent Factory in China(Analytical reagent). Phosalone standard (100 μg/mL, 1 mL) was obtained from the Agro-Environmental Protection Institute of China. The 2:3 methanol-water solution was prepared by mixing 40 mL of methanol and 60 mL of water. All water used in the experiments was deionized water.

A UH5300 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) and an LS55 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, USA) were applied, respectively, to record the UV-vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra. The QYs of CDs and N-CDs were measured through a HORIBA FluoroMax-4 spectrophotometer with a calibrated integrating sphere. A JEM-F200 high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, JEOL, Japan) was used to characterize the morphology and distribution of N-CDs. XRD spectrum was taken at Rigaku D/MAX 2500V diffraction meter (Rigaku, Japan). FT-IR spectrum was recorded on Nicolet iS10 Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Thermo Scientific, UK). XPS pattern was recorded on a Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi-technique surface analysis instrument (Thermo Fisher Scientific, UK).

Briefly, 0.01∼0.2 g cellobiose was mixed in 10 mL deionized water and then transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. The mixture was maintained at 140∼210 °C for 3∼12 h, and cooled down to room temperature naturally. After the reaction was finished, CDs solution with brown color was obtained. The as-prepared CDs were filtered with a 0.22 μm membrane filter to remove the unwanted solid substances, further purified by a dialysis bag (1000 Da, against water) for one day, and stored at 4°C for further use.

N-CDs were prepared through a one-step hydrothermal method with cellobiose as a carbon source and NH4HCO3 as a nitrogen source. Briefly, 0.2 g cellobiose and 1.5 g NH4HCO3 were mixed in 10 mL deionized water and then transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. The mixture was maintained at 180 °C for 4 h, and cool down to room temperature naturally. After the reaction was finished, the N-CDs solution with brown color was obtained. The as-prepared N-CDs were filtered with a 0.22 μm membrane filter to remove the unwanted solid substances, further purified by a dialysis bag (1000 Da, against water) for one day, diluted to 100 mL, stored at 4°C for further use.

For temperature sensing, 100 μL of N-CDs from its stock solution was added into a series of 5 mL colorimetric tubes, then diluted to scale with deionized water. After that, the tubes were incubated at 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 50, 60, 70, and 80 °C for 10 min. Finally, the corresponding fluorescence spectra were recorded through LS55 fluorescence spectroscopy. The excitation wavelengths for fluorescence emission spectra measurement were fixed at 230 nm and 320 nm, both slit width of excitation and emission were 5 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric detection of phosalone via the dual fluorescence centers of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: For the ratiometric detection of phosalone via the dual fluorescence center of N-CDs, 100 μL of the N-CDs from its stock solution was diluted into 2.5 mL with a 2:3 methanol-water solution. Then 0, 30, 50, 70, 100, 200, 400, 600, 1000, 1500, 2000, 3000 μL of 10 μg/mL phosalone standard solution was titrated into the N-CDs-methanol-water system. Mixed them up. After incubation of 1 min at room temperature, the fluorescence emission spectra with the excitation of 230 nm and 320 nm were recorded and used for quantitative analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric detection of phosalone via the dual absorption bands of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The typical ratiometric sensing procedure for phosalone was described as follows. 200 μL of N-CDs from its stock solution, and 0, 2, 5, 10, 15, 20, 50, 100, 200, 250, 300, 400, 500, 600, 700 μL of 10 μg/mL phosalone were put into a series tubes, then diluted to 5 mL with 2:3 methanol-water solution. The as-obtained solution was measured through a UH5300 spectrophotometer in the wavelength range 190∼400 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible formation mechanism of CDs and N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The plausible mechanism of cellobiose to both CDs and N-CDs was discussed firstly. Su and his co-worker found that with the hydrothermal treatment of cellulose, it progressively carbonized to CDs through the hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF) process [12]. To find out the formation mechanism of CDs and N-CDs, the UV-Vis spectra and fluorescence spectra of CDs without N-doping were investigated first. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the hydrothermal treatment of cellobiose led to the appearance of a strong absorption peak at 270 nm, which was the characteristic peak of HMF [23]. Simultaneously, as the temperature raised to 160°C, the characteristic peak of the sp2 carbon cluster of CDs appeared at around 230 nm [24], suggesting that some of the HMF were further carbonized into the sp2 carbon cluster. As the temperature rose from 160 °C to 200 °C, both the absorption peak at 230 nm and 270 nm were increased, and the fluorescence peak at 420 nm showed the same increase. As the temperature exceeds 200 °C, the absorption peak at 270 nm decreases, while the absorption peak at 230 nm and the fluorescence emission peak at 420 nm still keep increasing, indicating more HMF have carbonized into sp2 carbon cluster of CDs. When increasing the concentration of cellobiose, the same increase of the absorption peak at 230 nm and 270 nm, and the fluorescence emission peak at 420 nm was found (Fig. 1B). The above results show that with the hydrothermal treatment of cellobiose, the HMF was formed firstly, continuous exposure of hydrothermal treatment caused aromatization of the condensation/polymerization, which subsequently led to the production of aromatic clusters. These carbon cores formed then burst out when the concentration of the clusters exceeded a critical supersaturation level and resulted in the formation of carbon dots decorated with the functional groups [25]. Therefore, with the increase of reaction temperature and the amount of carbon source, more and more carbon dot was formed, which led to the increase of the absorption at 207 nm and the fluorescence emission at 420 nm.

An increase in temperature leads to the formation of the sp2 carbon cluster, which is consistent with the result from Lu et al [26]. In their study, CDs showed the strongest under hydrothermal at 150 °C. In which, large molecules or crosslinked polymer chains were dominant rather than carbonized structures. However, in this study, the fluorescence of N-CDs showed an increase trend with the increase of temperature, which means that sp2 carbon clusters dominate the fluorescence of CDs. This may be because HMF based large molecules or polymers cannot emit fluorescence. Therefore, destroying the balance between HMF and sp2 carbon clusters, inducing the condensation/polymerization of the carbon cluster is the main means to improve the fluorescence intensity of CDs.

According to the above results, inducing the condensation/polymerization of the carbon cluster is an efficient way to form CDs. We tried to enhance the reaction time from 3 h to 12 h, while the reaction almost reached equilibrium within 6 h, and the fluorescence and absorption did not show significant change as reaction time prolonged from 6 to 12 h (Fig. 1C). Based on the AIE theory, Wang et al proposed a “molecular glue” strategy to produce a large-scale conversion of clustering-induced emission luminogen to carbon dots [22]. In their study, the weak fluorescence sodium alginate was “glued” together by ethylenediamine though generated amide bonds to large scale produce CDs. Inspired by this strategy, proper amounts of NH4HCO3 were added. As expected, at the presence of NH4HCO3, the absorption band at 270 nm was decreased sharply, while the absorption peak at 230 nm was blue-shifted to 207 nm and enhance sharply. Correspondingly, the fluorescence at 420 nm was increased significantly (Fig. 1D). Meanwhile, the reaction process was shorted from 6 h to 4 h, and the optimal temperature was decreased from 210°C to 180°C. Based on the AIE theory, the aromatic carbon clusters have been “glued” to form CDs. In the “glued” process, aromatic carbon was constantly consumed, which destroys the transformation balance between HMF and aromatic carbon and made HMF continuously transform into aromatic carbon. Therefore, the absorption at 270 nm was reduced, while the generation of more and more CDs led to the significant enhancement of the fluorescence peak at 420 nm. The possible formation mechanism for CDs and N-CDs was illustrated in Fig. 1E.

The significant fluorescence enhancement of CDs induced by N doping is well known [27, 28]. However, most studies revealed that this can be ascribed to the changes of chemical or electronic structure induced by N doping [18, 28-31]. According to Liu's study [18], heteroatomic doping can produce more surface defects for electron transfer and radiative recombination, thus strengthening the fluorescence properties. N doping might reduce the bandgap. Moreover, sp2 C can link O- or N-containing functional groups and possibly dominate the intrinsic fluorescence of CDs. Besides, the synergistic effect between sp2 C and N-doped surface states might also be ascribed as the reason for the fluorescence strengthen. This study agrees with the three fluorescence enhancement reasons mentioned by Liu et al. Moreover, this study proposes that the “glued” function of N promotes the carbonization reaction of cellobiose in a positive direction, thus generating more CDs. The significant increase in the generation yield of CDs is also an important reason for the fluorescence enhancement. Due to its excellent optical property, this paper mainly discusses N-CDs.

Morphology and distribution of the as-prepared N-CDs were characterized by TEM. The TEM image (Fig. 2 A) clearly showed good disperse spherical nanoparticles. The inset of Fig. 2A showed the HRTEM image of N-CDs, it was evidence that N-CDs have a crystalline structure, and the crystal distance is 0.23 nm, which could be signed to the (100) facet of graphite [32, 33]. The size distribution of the as-prepared N-CDs, which was obtained by counting of 100 N-CDs particles, was in a narrow scope of 1.3∼4.8 nm, and the average diameter was found to be 2.8 nm, indicating that the N-CDs were nearly uniform in size. The HRTEM results imply that the as-prepared N-CDs contain graphite-like carbon, which can be further confirmed by the XRD pattern. According to Fig. 2B, the XRD pattern of the N-CDs revealed two distinct peaks at a 2θ value of 25.6° and 43.5°corresponding respectively to (002) and (101) planes of graphitic carbon [34]. The peak at 25.6° corresponded to (002) graphitic plane, which was definitely more separated than pure graphite indicating the high exfoliated graphitic nature of the material [35].

The chemical structure of N-CDs was further performed by FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2C). The presence of the typical stretching vibration of C=C/C=N at 1603 cm−1, the stretching vibration, and out-plane bending vibration of aromatic C-H at 3200 cm−1, and 863 cm−1 confirmed the sp2 structure aromatic carbon clusters at the as-prepared N-CDs [36]. Moreover, the characterized peaks at 3200 cm−1, 1603 cm−1, 1412 cm−1, and 901 cm−1 suggested the existence of amino nitrogen and aromatic nitrogen structures [37]. The presence of C=N and aromatic nitrogen structures imply that some of the sp2 aromatic carbon have been replaced by nitrogen. In other word, nitrogen has been doped into N-CDs successfully. Besides, oxygen-containing groups were also found, including asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of C-O give rise to bands at 1163 cm−1 ∼ 910 cm−1, O-H stretching vibrations of the carboxyl and hydroxyl at 3395 cm−1 [38].

The absorption spectrum, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the cellobiose-derived N-CDs were illustrated in Fig. 2D. The N-CDs showed dual absorption bands at 207 nm and 270 nm (curve a), which were ascribed to π–π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains and n–π* conjugation of the surface functional groups [2, 14], respectively. Correspondingly, dual excitation bands at 230 nm and 320 nm were found (curve b). Almost the same as cellulose-derived N-CDs [14], no matter excited by 230 nm or 320 nm, only one emission band at 420 nm was observed for the cellobiose-derived N-CDs (curve c and d). The fluorescence centers obtained by the excitation wavelength at 230 nm and 320 nm might correspond to conjugated carbon atoms and O-containing, N-containing functional groups [2, 14, 39], respectively. When excited by 230 nm, a large stokes shift up to 190 nm (19669 cm−1) was obtained by N-CDs. The diluted N-CDs show pale yellow color under natural light and radiated bright blue color under 365 nm UV light (Inset of Fig. 2D). The above results suggest the same fluorescence mechanism for the CDs and N-CDs derived from cellulose and cellobiose.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the CDs derived from cellulose and cellobiose
ce:section.ce:para: Cellobiose is a hydrolysis product of cellulose. Cellulose possesses polydisperse linear glucose polymer chains and the formation of hydrogen-bonded supramolecular structures. Our previous study [14] indicated that compared with the supramolecular structure cellulose, the low-molecular carbohydrate are more likely to be carbonized into CDs. Therefore, the comparison of the synthesis condition between the CDs derived from supramolecular structure cellulose and simple molecular cellobiose is necessary. According to Table S1 in the supporting information, sulphuric acid carbonizing and hydrothermal carbonizing are the main methods for deriving CDs from cellulose. Though sulphuric acid carbonizing does not require high temperature, it needs strong sulphuric acid, which is not consistent with the idea of “green chemistry”. Besides, these methods also need a complex operations and suffer from the drawback of low quantum yield. The hydrothermal carbonization of cellulose usually needs a temperature higher than 200°C and hydrothermal time longer than 12 h.

In our previous study, hydrothermal carbonization of microcrystalline cellulose into N-CDs required 14 h of reaction at 210°C. After the reaction, many unreacted cellulose residues can still be observed [14]. For carbonization of cellobiose into CDs, it only needs 6 h hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C without N-doping. With the doping of N atoms, both hydrothermal time and temperature were decrease and only cost 4 h hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C. In summer, CDs and N-CDs derived from cellobiose have almost the same fluorescence properties as those derived from cellulose. However, the cellobiose-derived CDs and N-CDs require a shorter carbonization time, lower hydrothermal temperature, especially N-CDs. Since the hydrolysis of cellulose to cellobiose has been a mature technique, converting cellulose to cellobiose or other low-molecular carbohydrates can synthesize CDs under relatively facile conditions and can obtain a higher yield. This might be a feasible strategy for CDs synthesis from cellulose.

The increase of the temperature results in the activates of the non-radiative channel, which leads to more exciting electrons back to the ground state via non-radiative transition, thus quenching the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs [30, 40] Fig. 3. A and B showed that as the temperature increased from 10 °C to 80 °C, both the fluorescence centers at the excitation of 230 nm and 320 nm decreased gradually, suggesting the distinguishable temperature-dependent fluorescence. Therefore, an N-CDs-based fluorescence probe for temperature sensing was constructed. For temperature sensing, fluorescence-based nano-sensor has advantages of fast response, no influencing the original temperature, high spatial resolution, and safety of remote handing, thus causing more and more attention. Compared with other fluorescence-based nano temperature fluorescence sensor, the fluorescence temperature sensor based on CDs show obvious advantages. Because CDs is of excellent properties, such as green and facile to the object and environment, heavy metal free, easy to be prepared, cheap, and excellent fluorescence properties, and so on. More importantly, CDs-based fluorescence temperature sensor could be used as nanoprobes in liquids.

Noteworthy, no matter excited by 230 nm or 320 nm, the N-CDs all yielded an emission centered at around 420 nm at different temperatures. No wavelength shift and no full width at half maximum (FWHM) change were measured. As illustrated in Fig. 3C, the fluorescence quenching ratios satisfy the linear equation of Fi/F10= 1.04-0.0046T (R=0.9993) and Fi/F10=1.07-0.0053T (R=0.9985) for the excitation of 230 nm and 320 nm, respectively. In which, Fi/F10 is the ratio of 10 °C and i °C, respectively. To test the reusability of the as-proposed fluorescence thermometers, the fluorescence of the N-CDs excited by 230 nm and 320 nm were recorded over four heating (80 °C) and cooling (10 °C) cycles (Fig. 3D). In each cycle, the fluorescence was recorded after 10 min incubation for thermal equilibration. Results showed that the fluorescence of as-prepared N-CDs was reversible and recoverable, and in the temperature range of 10∼80 °C its fluorescence centers were not destructed. The good linearity and the reusability of the N-CDs ensure its potential application in temperature sensing.

Phosalone is a very common insecticide and acaricide for various farmland to control caterpillars and beetles. It has a high toxic to aquatic organisms and also has a highly bioconcentration factor [41]. Overview of the detection assays for phosalone (See Table S2 in the supporting information), chromatography and mass spectrometry based techniques are the major method. Though exhibited good detection performance, they suffer from the drawback of time-consuming, costly, and require complicated instrumentation. Therefore, it is an urge to develop a rapid method to measure the concentration of phosalone, especially in emergency food safety incidents.

Fig. 4 A and B displayed that as phosalone addition, the fluorescence center with the excitation wavelength of 230 nm (Origins from sp2 conjugated carbon core) showed an efficiency quenching, while the fluorescence center with the excitation wavelength of 320 nm (Origins from O-containing and N-containing functional groups) just showed little change. The dual fluorescence centers of N-CDs revealed different responses to phosalone, which promised its potential application in constructing the novel ratiometric probe for phosalone detection. Besides, as shown in Fig. 4C, both absorption bands of N-CDs at 207 nm and 270 nm almost overlap with phosalone. Therefore, we speculated that the mixing of N-CDs and phosalone might lead to the increase of both absorption bands. However, we were surprised to observe that, with the addition of phosalone, the absorption peak at 207 nm (Corresponding to the sp2 conjugated carbon core) decreased gradually, and the absorption peak at 270 nm (Corresponding to the O-containing and N-containing functional groups) increased gradually (Fig. 4D). The decrease in the absorption band at 207 nm may be cause by the interaction between N-CDs and phosalone, and the increase of the absorption band at 270 nm may be caused by the spectra overlap between N-CDs and phosalone. Therefore, the decrease of the absorption at 207 nm and the increase of the absorption at 270 nm promised a reverse-change signal type of ratiometric probe for phosalone detection. Though the well-defined absorption feature of phosalone ensures that it can be directly detected by measuring its absorption spectra, the interaction between N-CDs can increase the detection sensitivity and the construction of a ratiometric probe can increase the detection accuracy.

The efficiency fluorescence quenching of 230 nm and the absorption decrease at 207 nm indicated that phosalone mainly interacted with the graphite-like sp2 carbon cluster, not with the surface functional group. A relatively strong hydrophobicity of the phosalone determines that it will interact with graphite-like sp2 carbon clusters, which are also hydrophobic, rather than with the surface hydrophilic functional groups. Meanwhile, Zeta potential measurement result showed that the phosalone possesses negative potential (-3.05 eV), which indicated that the phosalone would interact with the positive charge. The Zeta potential of N-CDs was measured to be -9.34 eV, indicating that N-CDs were mainly surrounded by the negative functional groups, such as -OH, and -COOH. N atoms doped into the sp2 carbon cluster and existed in the forms of pyridine nitrogen, pyrrole nitrogen, and graphite nitrogen [33], which have abundant positive charges, would provide suitable binding sites for phosalone. The more negative potential of the N-CDs-phosalone system (-16.3 mV) further demonstrated that the phosalone was in interaction with the positive charges nitrogen.

The sensitivity of the fluorescence ratiometric and colorimetric dual modes detection of phosalone was investigated. Fig. 5.A displayed the resultant relationship of I320/I230 versus phosalone concentration from 0.00∼5.45 μg/mL, where I320 and I230 were the fluorescence of N-CDs excited by 320 nm and 230 nm at the presence of different phosalone concentrations, respectively. I320/I230 displayed a good linear relationship toward the phosalone concentration (ρ) in the ranges of 0.12 to 2.86 μg/mL (R=0.9993) and 2.86 to 5.45 μg/mL (R=0.9990). The linear equations were I320/I230=0.34ρ+0.72 and I320/I230=0.65ρ-0.18, respectively. LOD was estimated to be 42.90 ng/mL according to the 3δ/k principle (Where δ was the standard deviation of blank signal (n=10), and k was the slope of the calibration). A270/A207 versus phosalone levels from 0.00 to 1.80 μg/mL was also illustrated in Fig. 5B, where A270 and A207 were the absorbances of N-CDs at 270 nm and 207 nm at the presence of different phosalone concentrations, respectively. There was a linear relationship between the ratios of A270/A207 and the concentrations of phosalone ranging from 0.02∼1.40 μg/mL. The linear regression for phosalone detection was A270/A207=1.15ρ+0.29, (R=0.9993), and the LOD was calculated to be 6.67 ng/mL according to the 3δ/k principle (Where δ was the standard deviation of blank signal (n=10), k was the slope of the calibration). However, when the concentration of phosalone was lower than 0.02 μg/mL or higher than 1.40 μg/mL, it didn't show linearity. The detection performance of phosalone of this work and the previous study was compared in Table S2 in supporting information. According to Table S2, the detection performance of the proposed dual fluorescence center-based ratiometric assay and absorption-based ratiometric assay were comparable with the reported methods.

Herein, N-CDs were synthesized from cellobiose, which is the dimer of glucose and the hydrolysis product of cellulose, via a simple hydrothermal method. The formation, characterization, and potential applications of the as-prepared N-CDs were discussed in detail. The hydrothermal treatment of cellobiose led to the formation of HMF and further carbonized sp2 carbon clusters and formed N-CDs. The reaction balance between the sp2 carbon cluster and HMF limited more cellobiose transform into N-CDs. The doping of nitrogen could act as “molecule glue”, more and more sp2 carbon clusters were “glued” together to formed N-CDs. The balance between the sp2 carbon cluster and HMF was destroyed, more and more HMF was transferred into the sp2 carbon cluster then further transferred into N-CDs. Moreover, the temperature-dependent fluorescence of the N-CDs ensured its potential application in temperature measurement in the range of 10∼80 °C. The as-prepared N-CDs possess dual fluorescence centers at the excitation of 230 nm and 320 nm and dual absorption bands at 207 nm and 270 nm. The addition of phosalone led to both the different responses of the dual fluorescence centers and dual absorption bands, thus outputting dual-mode ratiometric assays for phosalone detection.

Xiufen Liao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Project administration. Congjin Chen: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Peiwen Wang: Writing – review & editing, Data curation. Ruxia Zhou: Investigation, Writing – review & editing, Data curation. Xilian Zhao: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Hui Fan: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Zuqiang Huang: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31660183), Guangxi Natural Science Foundation (2015GXNSFAA139023), the Dean/Opening Project of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Petrochemical Resource Processing and Process Intensification Technology (2020K003), and the Scientific Research Foundation of Guangxi University (XJPZ160713).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.materresbull.2022.111790.
